item.title: Ep 1 · Ch 1 — Bond Not Bargain: Distress as Attachment Crisis
item.description:
Chapter 1: Making the Case for Effective Couple Therapy
Why EFT abandoned the quid-pro-quo, communication-coach model of couple therapy and reads distress through Bowlby's attachment lens instead. Listeners learn to hear reactive anger and withdrawal as secondary emotion masking primary attachment fear, and to reframe the negative cycle as the shared enemy.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 1: Making the Case for Effective Couple Therapy
Why EFT abandoned the quid-pro-quo, communication-coach model of couple therapy and reads distress through Bowlby's attachment lens instead. Listeners learn to hear reactive anger and withdrawal as secondary emotion masking primary attachment fear, and to reframe the negative cycle as the shared enemy.

item.title: Ep 2 · Ch 1 — Nine Steps, Three Stages: Sequencing EFT Correctly
item.description:
Chapter 1: Making the Case for Effective Couple Therapy
A working map of EFT's nine steps across de-escalation, restructuring positions, and consolidation, plus why the sequence cannot be skipped. Covers the red flags of jumping to problem-solving too early or pushing an enactment before primary emotion has been deepened.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 1: Making the Case for Effective Couple Therapy
A working map of EFT's nine steps across de-escalation, restructuring positions, and consolidation, plus why the sequence cannot be skipped. Covers the red flags of jumping to problem-solving too early or pushing an enactment before primary emotion has been deepened.

item.title: Ep 3 · Ch 1 — Carol and Steve: Drawing Out the Silent Withdrawer
item.description:
Chapter 1: Making the Case for Effective Couple Therapy
A Stage 2 case walkthrough where the therapist stays with Steve's chest tightness and "dark cloud" rather than urging him to talk, expanding it into felt sadness and fear. Shows how processing the withdrawer's emerging vulnerability with the pursuer sets up the enactment that shifts the bond.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 1: Making the Case for Effective Couple Therapy
A Stage 2 case walkthrough where the therapist stays with Steve's chest tightness and "dark cloud" rather than urging him to talk, expanding it into felt sadness and fear. Shows how processing the withdrawer's emerging vulnerability with the pursuer sets up the enactment that shifts the bond.

item.title: Ep 4 · Ch 1 — Relapse After Breakthrough: Holding the Attachment Frame
item.description:
Chapter 1: Making the Case for Effective Couple Therapy
What to do when a couple blows up over parenting or money after real Stage 2 gains, without panicking or sliding into mediation. Teaches normalizing the slip, shifting from content back to process, and using the secure base to work old problems in a new way.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 1: Making the Case for Effective Couple Therapy
What to do when a couple blows up over parenting or money after real Stage 2 gains, without panicking or sliding into mediation. Teaches normalizing the slip, shifting from content back to process, and using the secure base to work old problems in a new way.

item.title: Ep 5 · Ch 1 — The Fix-It Compulsion: Regulating Yourself Mid-Session
item.description:
Chapter 1: Making the Case for Effective Couple Therapy
How a therapist's own attachment anxiety gets triggered by hostility in the room, and why the urge to psychoeducate or problem-solve is usually self-soothing in disguise. Offers concrete self-regulation moves for tolerating secondary anger and staying a secure base for both partners.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 1: Making the Case for Effective Couple Therapy
How a therapist's own attachment anxiety gets triggered by hostility in the room, and why the urge to psychoeducate or problem-solve is usually self-soothing in disguise. Offers concrete self-regulation moves for tolerating secondary anger and staying a secure base for both partners.

item.title: Ep 6 · Ch 2 — When Distance Reads as Danger: Love's Survival Code
item.description:
Chapter 2: Love: A Prototype for Relationship Change
Bowlby's attachment frame recast for couple therapy: isolation is coded by the brain as threat, so demanding and withdrawing are survival strategies, not skill deficits. Listeners learn why bottom-up corrective experience beats top-down emotion education, and how ARE (accessibility, responsiveness, engagement) redefines the goal from conflict containment to secure bonding.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 2: Love: A Prototype for Relationship Change
Bowlby's attachment frame recast for couple therapy: isolation is coded by the brain as threat, so demanding and withdrawing are survival strategies, not skill deficits. Listeners learn why bottom-up corrective experience beats top-down emotion education, and how ARE (accessibility, responsiveness, engagement) redefines the goal from conflict containment to secure bonding.

item.title: Ep 7 · Ch 2 — Making the Therapy Room a Safe Haven
item.description:
Chapter 2: Love: A Prototype for Relationship Change
How the therapist functions as a temporary secure base so partners can explore warded-off vulnerability, with validation as the non-negotiable first move. Includes red flags that you have lost the attachment lens, scripts for normalizing dependency needs, and how to regulate your own voice and pacing to lower the room's arousal.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 2: Love: A Prototype for Relationship Change
How the therapist functions as a temporary secure base so partners can explore warded-off vulnerability, with validation as the non-negotiable first move. Includes red flags that you have lost the attachment lens, scripts for normalizing dependency needs, and how to regulate your own voice and pacing to lower the room's arousal.

item.title: Ep 8 · Ch 2 — James and Margaret: Depression as Separation Distress
item.description:
Chapter 2: Love: A Prototype for Relationship Change
A case walkthrough of empty-nesters caught in demand-distance turning to mutual withdrawal, where James's 'chronic depression' is reframed as the hopelessness of an unreachable attachment figure and Margaret's 'silent seethe' as shame about needing closeness. Listeners see the therapist follow the tears, block the escalation, and track the cycle until her resentment turns to compassion.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 2: Love: A Prototype for Relationship Change
A case walkthrough of empty-nesters caught in demand-distance turning to mutual withdrawal, where James's 'chronic depression' is reframed as the hopelessness of an unreachable attachment figure and Margaret's 'silent seethe' as shame about needing closeness. Listeners see the therapist follow the tears, block the escalation, and track the cycle until her resentment turns to compassion.

item.title: Ep 9 · Ch 2 — Catching the Bullet: Intercepting Contempt Without Shaming
item.description:
Chapter 2: Love: A Prototype for Relationship Change
The EFT move for hostile, shaming comments in session: block the attack, reframe it as the anger of despair, and validate the attacker's fear so the alliance survives. Covers the twin failure modes of sitting silent while one partner is eviscerated and siding with the 'victim' by labeling the critic abusive.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 2: Love: A Prototype for Relationship Change
The EFT move for hostile, shaming comments in session: block the attack, reframe it as the anger of despair, and validate the attacker's fear so the alliance survives. Covers the twin failure modes of sitting silent while one partner is eviscerated and siding with the 'victim' by labeling the critic abusive.

item.title: Ep 10 · Ch 2 — Staying a Secure Base When Clients Attack You
item.description:
Chapter 2: Love: A Prototype for Relationship Change
What to do with your own triggered defenses when a client accuses you of taking sides or calling him abusive: stop, validate his reality, and clarify rather than argue. Explores how the therapist's own attachment security supports empathy under fire, and how to normalize losing your relational skills when the bond is threatened.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 2: Love: A Prototype for Relationship Change
What to do with your own triggered defenses when a client accuses you of taking sides or calling him abusive: stop, validate his reality, and clarify rather than argue. Explores how the therapist's own attachment security supports empathy under fire, and how to normalize losing your relational skills when the bond is threatened.

item.title: Ep 11 · Ch 3 — Primary, Secondary, Instrumental: Emotion as Attachment Compass
item.description:
Chapter 3: Three New Insights Into Change in Emotionally Focused Couple Therapy
Distinguishes the three types of emotion in EFT and why couples stay stuck arguing from secondary reactivity. Explains processing raw attachment affect both within a partner and in-between the couple, rather than talking about feelings.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 3: Three New Insights Into Change in Emotionally Focused Couple Therapy
Distinguishes the three types of emotion in EFT and why couples stay stuck arguing from secondary reactivity. Explains processing raw attachment affect both within a partner and in-between the couple, rather than talking about feelings.

item.title: Ep 12 · Ch 3 — The Six-Step Sequence for Deepening and Sharing Affect
item.description:
Chapter 3: Three New Insights Into Change in Emotionally Focused Couple Therapy
Walks through Bradley's generic sequence from distilling attachment affect to structuring congruent responses and integrating them. Covers heightening, evocative responding, and why an enactment falls flat if you stage it before the primary emotion is fully alive.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 3: Three New Insights Into Change in Emotionally Focused Couple Therapy
Walks through Bradley's generic sequence from distilling attachment affect to structuring congruent responses and integrating them. Covers heightening, evocative responding, and why an enactment falls flat if you stage it before the primary emotion is fully alive.

item.title: Ep 13 · Ch 3 — Joe and Rebecca: Reframing Nagging as a Cry
item.description:
Chapter 3: Three New Insights Into Change in Emotionally Focused Couple Therapy
Traces a session where the therapist refuses the chore-negotiation bait and reframes Rebecca's nagging as a plea for Joe. Shows how highlighting the pursuer's fear pulls the withdrawer's caregiving system online instead of triggering more defense.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 3: Three New Insights Into Change in Emotionally Focused Couple Therapy
Traces a session where the therapist refuses the chore-negotiation bait and reframes Rebecca's nagging as a plea for Joe. Shows how highlighting the pursuer's fear pulls the withdrawer's caregiving system online instead of triggering more defense.

item.title: Ep 14 · Ch 3 — Spotting Instrumental Emotion Used to Derail Sessions
item.description:
Chapter 3: Three New Insights Into Change in Emotionally Focused Couple Therapy
Teaches how to tell a tactical emotional display from genuine primary or secondary emotion, using Robert's performed frustration as the example. Gives scripts to step through the smoke bomb, hold the frame, and return to the panic it was hiding.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 3: Three New Insights Into Change in Emotionally Focused Couple Therapy
Teaches how to tell a tactical emotional display from genuine primary or secondary emotion, using Robert's performed frustration as the example. Gives scripts to step through the smoke bomb, hold the frame, and return to the panic it was hiding.

item.title: Ep 15 · Ch 3 — Using Your Own Body to Find Warded-Off Terror
item.description:
Chapter 3: Three New Insights Into Change in Emotionally Focused Couple Therapy
Shows the therapist searching their own affective experience when a client laughs off attachment threat, then offering that feeling back tentatively in the first person. Covers heightening the incongruence rather than colluding with minimization.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 3: Three New Insights Into Change in Emotionally Focused Couple Therapy
Shows the therapist searching their own affective experience when a client laughs off attachment threat, then offering that feeling back tentatively in the first person. Covers heightening the incongruence rather than colluding with minimization.

item.title: Ep 16 · Ch 4 — Depression as the Loss of Secure Connection
item.description:
Chapter 4: Depression: Enemy of the Attachment Bond
Reframes depression from an individual chemical imbalance into a systemic response to attachment loss, using Coyne's interactional model to show how cheer-up attempts, withdrawal, and deepening hopelessness feed each other. Listeners learn why secure bonding interactions function as a neurobiological antidote to depressive isolation.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 4: Depression: Enemy of the Attachment Bond
Reframes depression from an individual chemical imbalance into a systemic response to attachment loss, using Coyne's interactional model to show how cheer-up attempts, withdrawal, and deepening hopelessness feed each other. Listeners learn why secure bonding interactions function as a neurobiological antidote to depressive isolation.

item.title: Ep 17 · Ch 4 — Making Depression the Couple's Shared Enemy
item.description:
Chapter 4: Depression: Enemy of the Attachment Bond
A practical playbook for folding a major depression diagnosis into the negative cycle without shaming the depressed partner or blaming the well partner. Covers the externalization script, mapping symptoms onto attachment fears, and shifting the non-depressed partner from fixing to staying emotionally present.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 4: Depression: Enemy of the Attachment Bond
A practical playbook for folding a major depression diagnosis into the negative cycle without shaming the depressed partner or blaming the well partner. Covers the externalization script, mapping symptoms onto attachment fears, and shifting the non-depressed partner from fixing to staying emotionally present.

item.title: Ep 18 · Ch 4 — Terry and Mike: Infidelity, Pursuit, and Despair
item.description:
Chapter 4: Depression: Enemy of the Attachment Bond
Walks through a couple where recurrent depression rides on a pursuer-withdrawer cycle compounded by past infidelity, tracing how Mike's retreat confirms Terry's fear that she is not enough. Shows the enactment where the withdrawer's own fear of losing her, not his problem-solving, de-escalates the cycle.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 4: Depression: Enemy of the Attachment Bond
Walks through a couple where recurrent depression rides on a pursuer-withdrawer cycle compounded by past infidelity, tracing how Mike's retreat confirms Terry's fear that she is not enough. Shows the enactment where the withdrawer's own fear of losing her, not his problem-solving, de-escalates the cycle.

item.title: Ep 19 · Ch 4 — Modulating Affect and Assessing Risk in Session
item.description:
Chapter 4: Depression: Enemy of the Attachment Bond
How to titrate emotional intensity when a depressed partner's window of tolerance is narrow: intercepting a venting partner, translating frustration into attachment protest, and never ending a session at peak arousal. Includes direct suicidality assessment as non-negotiable standard of care alongside the systemic work.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 4: Depression: Enemy of the Attachment Bond
How to titrate emotional intensity when a depressed partner's window of tolerance is narrow: intercepting a venting partner, translating frustration into attachment protest, and never ending a session at peak arousal. Includes direct suicidality assessment as non-negotiable standard of care alongside the systemic work.

item.title: Ep 20 · Ch 5 — Aphasia as Attachment Trauma: The Withdraw-Withdraw Trap
item.description:
Chapter 5: Living With Aphasia
How stroke-induced aphasia attacks the medium a couple uses to stay bonded, activating attachment and caregiving systems at once and locking partners into mutual withdrawal. Listeners learn to read lack of initiation and catastrophic reactions as neurological symptoms rather than relational rejection, and why an EFT/speech-language cotherapy pairing is often required.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 5: Living With Aphasia
How stroke-induced aphasia attacks the medium a couple uses to stay bonded, activating attachment and caregiving systems at once and locking partners into mutual withdrawal. Listeners learn to read lack of initiation and catastrophic reactions as neurological symptoms rather than relational rejection, and why an EFT/speech-language cotherapy pairing is often required.

item.title: Ep 21 · Ch 5 — Building a Communication Platform With Markers and Scales
item.description:
Chapter 5: Living With Aphasia
A practical playbook for making EFT possible when one partner cannot speak: whiteboards, stick figures, emotion faces, visual analog scales, timelines, and the RISSSC technique used to level the conversational playing field. Listeners get the in-room moves for slowing down, checking meaning without assuming it, and containing catastrophic reactions.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 5: Living With Aphasia
A practical playbook for making EFT possible when one partner cannot speak: whiteboards, stick figures, emotion faces, visual analog scales, timelines, and the RISSSC technique used to level the conversational playing field. Listeners get the in-room moves for slowing down, checking meaning without assuming it, and containing catastrophic reactions.

item.title: Ep 22 · Ch 5 — Roger and Jane: The Cup With a Hole
item.description:
Chapter 5: Living With Aphasia
A stroke survivor with severe aphasia and explosive anger, and a burned-out wife on the brink of separation, worked through externalizing tools like the "Stroke Channel" TV and a drawn cup of joy. Listeners see how one nonverbal act of vulnerability - Roger drawing a hole in his cup - reframed rage as grief and softened his partner toward him.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 5: Living With Aphasia
A stroke survivor with severe aphasia and explosive anger, and a burned-out wife on the brink of separation, worked through externalizing tools like the "Stroke Channel" TV and a drawn cup of joy. Listeners see how one nonverbal act of vulnerability - Roger drawing a hole in his cup - reframed rage as grief and softened his partner toward him.

item.title: Ep 23 · Ch 6 — When Protective Buffering Leaves Her Alone
item.description:
Chapter 6: Medical Illness: Working With the Aftermath of Breast Cancer
Breast cancer activates both the attachment and caregiving systems, and when a husband's "protector role" turns into protective buffering, his suppressed fear reads as rejection and fuels his partner's angry pursuit. Listeners learn to treat post-diagnosis depression and anxiety not as individual pathology but as unmet attachment needs under existential threat, and to use attachment as a theory of affect regulation.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 6: Medical Illness: Working With the Aftermath of Breast Cancer
Breast cancer activates both the attachment and caregiving systems, and when a husband's "protector role" turns into protective buffering, his suppressed fear reads as rejection and fuels his partner's angry pursuit. Listeners learn to treat post-diagnosis depression and anxiety not as individual pathology but as unmet attachment needs under existential threat, and to use attachment as a theory of affect regulation.

item.title: Ep 24 · Ch 6 — Cassy's Starvation Diet and the Missed Chemotherapy
item.description:
Chapter 6: Medical Illness: Working With the Aftermath of Breast Cancer
A fearful-avoidant survivor who runs five miles a day and won't be touched, and a withdrawer who hides in the basement, are anchored by one unforgiven injury: Mike's absence at her first chemo session, which she had told him she didn't need. The episode traces how reframing hyper-independence as a "starvation diet" and surfacing Mike's own unprocessed grief over his sister's death converts abandonment into shared trauma.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 6: Medical Illness: Working With the Aftermath of Breast Cancer
A fearful-avoidant survivor who runs five miles a day and won't be touched, and a withdrawer who hides in the basement, are anchored by one unforgiven injury: Mike's absence at her first chemo session, which she had told him she didn't need. The episode traces how reframing hyper-independence as a "starvation diet" and surfacing Mike's own unprocessed grief over his sister's death converts abandonment into shared trauma.

item.title: Ep 25 · Ch 6 — Staging Forgiveness for a Medical-Trauma Injury
item.description:
Chapter 6: Medical Illness: Working With the Aftermath of Breast Cancer
A practical sequence for repairing an attachment injury born of illness: name the single moment of aloneness, anticipate that the injured partner will sabotage the first repair attempt, and block the sarcastic attack before it lands on newly disclosed vulnerability. Includes the red flags — attempting forgiveness before de-escalation, and settling for "I'm sorry you felt that way."
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 6: Medical Illness: Working With the Aftermath of Breast Cancer
A practical sequence for repairing an attachment injury born of illness: name the single moment of aloneness, anticipate that the injured partner will sabotage the first repair attempt, and block the sarcastic attack before it lands on newly disclosed vulnerability. Includes the red flags — attempting forgiveness before de-escalation, and settling for "I'm sorry you felt that way."

item.title: Ep 26 · Ch 7 — Why Numbing Keeps First Responders From Their Safe Haven
item.description:
Chapter 7: EFT With Couples Who Stand in Harm's Way
Listeners learn why emotional numbing — the survival skill that keeps firefighters, police, and soldiers alive — is the most attachment-destructive PTSD symptom, cutting the survivor off from the partner who could help them heal. The episode covers reframing the relationship as the victim and the trauma as the villain, and delivering psychoeducation experientially, in the moment a symptom surfaces.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 7: EFT With Couples Who Stand in Harm's Way
Listeners learn why emotional numbing — the survival skill that keeps firefighters, police, and soldiers alive — is the most attachment-destructive PTSD symptom, cutting the survivor off from the partner who could help them heal. The episode covers reframing the relationship as the victim and the trauma as the villain, and delivering psychoeducation experientially, in the moment a symptom surfaces.

item.title: Ep 27 · Ch 7 — A 9/11 Firefighter's Silence and His Wife's Despair
item.description:
Chapter 7: EFT With Couples Who Stand in Harm's Way
A session-by-session walk through Paul, an NYC firefighter with PTSD who refuses to speak about the World Trade Center collapse, and Maureen, whose depression and criticism grow as he retreats. Listeners see how honoring Paul's numbing as a profound skill builds trust with a wary first responder, and why it takes fifteen sessions before he lets his wife see his terror.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 7: EFT With Couples Who Stand in Harm's Way
A session-by-session walk through Paul, an NYC firefighter with PTSD who refuses to speak about the World Trade Center collapse, and Maureen, whose depression and criticism grow as he retreats. Listeners see how honoring Paul's numbing as a profound skill builds trust with a wary first responder, and why it takes fifteen sessions before he lets his wife see his terror.

item.title: Ep 28 · Ch 7 — Pacing Vulnerability in Micro-Steps With Trauma Couples
item.description:
Chapter 7: EFT With Couples Who Stand in Harm's Way
A practical playbook for "slicing the risk thinner" — asking a traumatized partner to share the fear of disclosure before the disclosure itself — plus how to ground a panic spike or explosive rage before it secondarily traumatizes the spouse. Includes contraindication screening, dissociation red flags, and scripts for containing the storm.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 7: EFT With Couples Who Stand in Harm's Way
A practical playbook for "slicing the risk thinner" — asking a traumatized partner to share the fear of disclosure before the disclosure itself — plus how to ground a panic spike or explosive rage before it secondarily traumatizes the spouse. Includes contraindication screening, dissociation red flags, and scripts for containing the storm.

item.title: Ep 29 · Ch 8 — Addiction as Faux Attachment, Not Physical Dependence
item.description:
Chapter 8: Addiction
Reframes addiction through the positive-incentive model and attachment theory: the substance or behavior functions as a controllable substitute safe haven for someone starved of secure connection. Listeners learn how addiction and relationship distress feed one escalating cycle, and why a secure bond—not abstinence alone—is the real antidote.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 8: Addiction
Reframes addiction through the positive-incentive model and attachment theory: the substance or behavior functions as a controllable substitute safe haven for someone starved of secure connection. Listeners learn how addiction and relationship distress feed one escalating cycle, and why a secure bond—not abstinence alone—is the real antidote.

item.title: Ep 30 · Ch 8 — Balancing the Canoe: Containment Before Deeper Vulnerability
item.description:
Chapter 8: Addiction
A practical playbook for running EFT when addiction is active: assess containment first, map the addiction directly into the couple's negative cycle, and catch the bullet when the partner attacks a newly vulnerable disclosure. Includes red flags for proceeding conjointly and steps for structuring the addict's alternative reach to the partner.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 8: Addiction
A practical playbook for running EFT when addiction is active: assess containment first, map the addiction directly into the couple's negative cycle, and catch the bullet when the partner attacks a newly vulnerable disclosure. Includes red flags for proceeding conjointly and steps for structuring the addict's alternative reach to the partner.

item.title: Ep 31 · Ch 8 — Louis and Peter: Poker, Vicodin, and Emotional Starvation
item.description:
Chapter 8: Addiction
Two cases show fearful-avoidant partners using poker and prescription pills as predictable substitutes for human comfort—Louis choosing a winning hand over his injured son, Peter numbing terror of abandonment. Listeners see how the therapist bypasses the content of the addiction, validates its function, and walks the couple through an attachment injury repair.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 8: Addiction
Two cases show fearful-avoidant partners using poker and prescription pills as predictable substitutes for human comfort—Louis choosing a winning hand over his injured son, Peter numbing terror of abandonment. Listeners see how the therapist bypasses the content of the addiction, validates its function, and walks the couple through an attachment injury repair.

item.title: Ep 32 · Ch 9 — Attachment, Arousal, and Sex as Safe Adventure
item.description:
Chapter 9: An Integrated Model of Couple and Sex Therapy
Explains how the attachment, caregiving, and sexual systems interlock, so that anxious partners hyperactivate sex as a bid for reassurance while avoidant partners deactivate into mechanical, emotionally absent touch. Listeners learn to reframe low desire or lost arousal as protective, not defective, and to trace the couple's pursue-withdraw cycle straight into the bedroom.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 9: An Integrated Model of Couple and Sex Therapy
Explains how the attachment, caregiving, and sexual systems interlock, so that anxious partners hyperactivate sex as a bid for reassurance while avoidant partners deactivate into mechanical, emotionally absent touch. Listeners learn to reframe low desire or lost arousal as protective, not defective, and to trace the couple's pursue-withdraw cycle straight into the bedroom.

item.title: Ep 33 · Ch 9 — De-escalate First, Then Stage the Sexual Softening
item.description:
Chapter 9: An Integrated Model of Couple and Sex Therapy
A practical sequence for integrating sex therapy into EFT: complete Stage 1 de-escalation before touching the erotic realm, map the negative cycle as it plays out during sex, and use an intercourse ban framed as an attachment exercise when the bedroom is pressure-filled. Covers the Stage 2 sexual softening event, explicit assessment questions, and the therapist's own avoidance of sexual language as a red flag.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 9: An Integrated Model of Couple and Sex Therapy
A practical sequence for integrating sex therapy into EFT: complete Stage 1 de-escalation before touching the erotic realm, map the negative cycle as it plays out during sex, and use an intercourse ban framed as an attachment exercise when the bedroom is pressure-filled. Covers the Stage 2 sexual softening event, explicit assessment questions, and the therapist's own avoidance of sexual language as a red flag.

item.title: Ep 34 · Ch 9 — Shutdown and Performance Fear: Two Bedroom Cycles
item.description:
Chapter 9: An Integrated Model of Couple and Sex Therapy
Two cases show sexual dysfunction as a systemic attachment response: Rhonda's body shuts down against Malcolm's sexual pressure and emotional absence, while Danny loses his erection under Jill's angry, anxious pursuit. Listeners see how the therapist moves Jill from secondary anger to the primary fear of not mattering, freeing Danny to name the terror of being on trial.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 9: An Integrated Model of Couple and Sex Therapy
Two cases show sexual dysfunction as a systemic attachment response: Rhonda's body shuts down against Malcolm's sexual pressure and emotional absence, while Danny loses his erection under Jill's angry, anxious pursuit. Listeners see how the therapist moves Jill from secondary anger to the primary fear of not mattering, freeing Danny to name the terror of being on trial.

item.title: Ep 35 · Ch 10 — Infidelity as Attachment Injury, Not Moral Breach
item.description:
Chapter 10: Infidelity
Reframes an affair as an interpersonal trauma that shatters the secure base, producing PTSD-like hypervigilance in the injured partner and shame-driven deactivation in the unfaithful one. Explains why behavioral contracts and password-sharing institutionalize hypervigilance instead of healing the wound, and how the injury surfaces as an impasse when Stage 2 reengagement is blocked.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 10: Infidelity
Reframes an affair as an interpersonal trauma that shatters the secure base, producing PTSD-like hypervigilance in the injured partner and shame-driven deactivation in the unfaithful one. Explains why behavioral contracts and password-sharing institutionalize hypervigilance instead of healing the wound, and how the injury surfaces as an impasse when Stage 2 reengagement is blocked.

item.title: Ep 36 · Ch 10 — When a Kiss Reignites the Betrayal Flashback
item.description:
Chapter 10: Infidelity
Follows John and Cindy through the beach incident, where a wandering glance triggers a flashback and an explosive enactment: her furious attack, his defensive urge to flee, and the therapist catching the bullet to reach the fear beneath the rage. Shows the move from defense to ownership as John finally stays present with the devastation he caused.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 10: Infidelity
Follows John and Cindy through the beach incident, where a wandering glance triggers a flashback and an explosive enactment: her furious attack, his defensive urge to flee, and the therapist catching the bullet to reach the fear beneath the rage. Shows the move from defense to ownership as John finally stays present with the devastation he caused.

item.title: Ep 37 · Ch 10 — Seven Steps to Resolve an Attachment Injury
item.description:
Chapter 10: Infidelity
Walks through the Attachment Injury Resolution Model step by step, from the injured partner's reactive account of the trauma to the healing reach and the caring antidote. Covers the red flags — premature apologies and running AIRM before de-escalation is complete — and the in-session moves for blocking defense and distilling a single core moment of pain.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 10: Infidelity
Walks through the Attachment Injury Resolution Model step by step, from the injured partner's reactive account of the trauma to the healing reach and the caring antidote. Covers the red flags — premature apologies and running AIRM before de-escalation is complete — and the in-session moves for blocking defense and distilling a single core moment of pain.

item.title: Ep 38 · Ch 11 — Stepfamilies Born of Loss: The Insider-Outsider Bind
item.description:
Chapter 11: Remarried Couples: Making New Connections and Facing Competing Attachments
Explains why remarriage is an attachment problem rather than a parenting problem: the stepparent enters as a historical outsider grasping for belonging through discipline, while the biological parent is trapped between a child's abandonment fear and a spouse's rejection fear. Listeners learn to reframe fights over house rules as the couple losing each other, and to name the stepparent's real terror of having no room in the home.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 11: Remarried Couples: Making New Connections and Facing Competing Attachments
Explains why remarriage is an attachment problem rather than a parenting problem: the stepparent enters as a historical outsider grasping for belonging through discipline, while the biological parent is trapped between a child's abandonment fear and a spouse's rejection fear. Listeners learn to reframe fights over house rules as the couple losing each other, and to name the stepparent's real terror of having no room in the home.

item.title: Ep 39 · Ch 11 — When the Six-Year-Old Becomes the Presenting Problem
item.description:
Chapter 11: Remarried Couples: Making New Connections and Facing Competing Attachments
Follows Mark and Jenny, newly remarried, who bring six-year-old Olivia to therapy as the identified patient while their own marriage quietly fractures. Listeners see the therapist decline the behavioral-chart trap, track Jenny's criticism as exclusion protest and Mark's withdrawal as divorce-driven guilt, and catch the sigh that opens Mark's sadness and his bid for Jenny to stand beside him.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 11: Remarried Couples: Making New Connections and Facing Competing Attachments
Follows Mark and Jenny, newly remarried, who bring six-year-old Olivia to therapy as the identified patient while their own marriage quietly fractures. Listeners see the therapist decline the behavioral-chart trap, track Jenny's criticism as exclusion protest and Mark's withdrawal as divorce-driven guilt, and catch the sigh that opens Mark's sadness and his bid for Jenny to stand beside him.

item.title: Ep 40 · Ch 11 — The Parent's Asymmetrical Reach: Sequencing EFFT Dyads
item.description:
Chapter 11: Remarried Couples: Making New Connections and Facing Competing Attachments
A working sequence for EFFT with remarried families: de-escalate the couple first, process the biological parent's guilt and defensiveness, then facilitate that parent's reach toward the child's grief, since a child is never asked to comfort an insecure parent. Covers de-triangulating an acting-out stepchild, coaching the stepparent out of positional power, and red flags such as full-family sessions too early or mediating custody disputes.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 11: Remarried Couples: Making New Connections and Facing Competing Attachments
A working sequence for EFFT with remarried families: de-escalate the couple first, process the biological parent's guilt and defensiveness, then facilitate that parent's reach toward the child's grief, since a child is never asked to comfort an insecure parent. Covers de-triangulating an acting-out stepchild, coaching the stepparent out of positional power, and red flags such as full-family sessions too early or mediating custody disputes.

item.title: Ep 41 · Ch 12 — Universal Attachment Needs, Culturally Coded Emotional Signals
item.description:
Chapter 12: Emotionally Focused Therapy With Culturally Diverse Couples
Why EFT holds up cross-culturally: the need for secure attachment is universal, but display rules and the meaning of emotions like guilt or shame are culturally constructed. Listeners learn to separate the attachment need from its signaling, and to read restraint or a mismatched smile as a regulation strategy rather than flat affect or lack of empathy.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 12: Emotionally Focused Therapy With Culturally Diverse Couples
Why EFT holds up cross-culturally: the need for secure attachment is universal, but display rules and the meaning of emotions like guilt or shame are culturally constructed. Listeners learn to separate the attachment need from its signaling, and to read restraint or a mismatched smile as a regulation strategy rather than flat affect or lack of empathy.

item.title: Ep 42 · Ch 12 — The Scissors, the Drain, and An-Ping's Superhero Burden
item.description:
Chapter 12: Emotionally Focused Therapy With Culturally Diverse Couples
A full walkthrough of Julie and An-Ping's cycle, where his practical problem-solving reads to her as dismissal and her requests read to him as an attack on his competence. The episode tracks the therapist's alliance repair after a rupture and the softening in which An-Ping names his terror of being seen as weak.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 12: Emotionally Focused Therapy With Culturally Diverse Couples
A full walkthrough of Julie and An-Ping's cycle, where his practical problem-solving reads to her as dismissal and her requests read to him as an attack on his competence. The episode tracks the therapist's alliance repair after a rupture and the softening in which An-Ping names his terror of being seen as weak.

item.title: Ep 43 · Ch 12 — Slicing Emotion Thinner When Vulnerability Feels Forbidden
item.description:
Chapter 12: Emotionally Focused Therapy With Culturally Diverse Couples
A practical playbook for cross-cultural EFT: scale emotion words down until they are culturally syntonic, use metaphors carefully, and validate display rules before bridging them for the partner. Includes the cultural genogram, immediate one-down alliance repair, and the Jorge case as a model for accessing vulnerability without demanding Western emotional labels.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 12: Emotionally Focused Therapy With Culturally Diverse Couples
A practical playbook for cross-cultural EFT: scale emotion words down until they are culturally syntonic, use metaphors carefully, and validate display rules before bridging them for the partner. Includes the cultural genogram, immediate one-down alliance repair, and the Jorge case as a model for accessing vulnerability without demanding Western emotional labels.

item.title: Ep 44 · Ch 13 — Sexual Identity Stress as an Attachment Threat
item.description:
Chapter 13: Lesbian Couples: Strong Identities, Strong Bonds
How chronic sexual identity-related stress (SIS) shapes models of self and other in gay and lesbian couples, turning the closet's survival strategies — invisibility, numbing, hypervigilance — into the fuel for the negative cycle. Listeners learn to pivot from reading a fight about hand-holding or family holidays as an intimacy mismatch to reading it as identity trauma.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 13: Lesbian Couples: Strong Identities, Strong Bonds
How chronic sexual identity-related stress (SIS) shapes models of self and other in gay and lesbian couples, turning the closet's survival strategies — invisibility, numbing, hypervigilance — into the fuel for the negative cycle. Listeners learn to pivot from reading a fight about hand-holding or family holidays as an intimacy mismatch to reading it as identity trauma.

item.title: Ep 45 · Ch 13 — Gabriela and Maxine: Panic Meets Practiced Invisibility
item.description:
Chapter 13: Lesbian Couples: Strong Identities, Strong Bonds
A pursue-withdraw cycle between a woman fearing disownment by her religious family and a lawyer who survives a homophobic workplace by disappearing, and how the therapist links Gabriela's desperate panic to internalized homonegativity rather than Maxine's distance alone. Includes the reframes and the softening reach that restore the safe haven.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 13: Lesbian Couples: Strong Identities, Strong Bonds
A pursue-withdraw cycle between a woman fearing disownment by her religious family and a lawyer who survives a homophobic workplace by disappearing, and how the therapist links Gabriela's desperate panic to internalized homonegativity rather than Maxine's distance alone. Includes the reframes and the softening reach that restore the safe haven.

item.title: Ep 46 · Ch 13 — Mapping Heterosexism Onto the Negative Cycle
item.description:
Chapter 13: Lesbian Couples: Strong Identities, Strong Bonds
A practical playbook for folding sexual identity history into Stage 1 assessment, spotting the 'out' discrepancy between partners, and validating dual-abandonment terror instead of pushing a client to come out. Covers red flags, therapist self-instructions, and four concrete steps for the next session.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 13: Lesbian Couples: Strong Identities, Strong Bonds
A practical playbook for folding sexual identity history into Stage 1 assessment, spotting the 'out' discrepancy between partners, and validating dual-abandonment terror instead of pushing a client to come out. Covers red flags, therapist self-instructions, and four concrete steps for the next session.

item.title: Ep 47 · Ch 14 — God as Attachment Figure: Where Faith Meets EFT
item.description:
Chapter 14: Spirituality and Emotionally Focused Couple Therapy: Exploring Common Ground
Why a client's relationship with the Divine functions as an attachment bond — a secure base that can be tapped, or an "accuser" that amplifies shame. Covers the convergence between EFT and Buddhist mindfulness (grasping and avoidance as anxious and avoidant attachment) and between the secure base and Christian covenant theology.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 14: Spirituality and Emotionally Focused Couple Therapy: Exploring Common Ground
Why a client's relationship with the Divine functions as an attachment bond — a secure base that can be tapped, or an "accuser" that amplifies shame. Covers the convergence between EFT and Buddhist mindfulness (grasping and avoidance as anxious and avoidant attachment) and between the secure base and Christian covenant theology.

item.title: Ep 48 · Ch 14 — The Pastor's Accuser: Shame That Blocks Repair
item.description:
Chapter 14: Spirituality and Emotionally Focused Couple Therapy: Exploring Common Ground
A Stage 2 case with a highly religious Christian couple, where a youth pastor's reactive anger collapses into spiritual conviction and withdrawal, leaving him doubly isolated from his wife and from God. Shows how the therapist externalizes the "accuser," stages an enactment of spiritual unworthiness, and lets the wife's grace consolidate the repair.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 14: Spirituality and Emotionally Focused Couple Therapy: Exploring Common Ground
A Stage 2 case with a highly religious Christian couple, where a youth pastor's reactive anger collapses into spiritual conviction and withdrawal, leaving him doubly isolated from his wife and from God. Shows how the therapist externalizes the "accuser," stages an enactment of spiritual unworthiness, and lets the wife's grace consolidate the repair.

item.title: Ep 49 · Ch 14 — Working With Faith Without a Theology Degree
item.description:
Chapter 14: Spirituality and Emotionally Focused Couple Therapy: Exploring Common Ground
A practical playbook for treating prayer and meditation as attachment behaviors rather than cognitive defenses — including the compensation-versus-correspondence pathways to divine attachment and how to pivot from theology to the felt sense. Includes the intake question, the spiritual-bypass move, and the "spiritually one-up" partner who triangulates God against the other.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 14: Spirituality and Emotionally Focused Couple Therapy: Exploring Common Ground
A practical playbook for treating prayer and meditation as attachment behaviors rather than cognitive defenses — including the compensation-versus-correspondence pathways to divine attachment and how to pivot from theology to the felt sense. Includes the intake question, the spiritual-bypass move, and the "spiritually one-up" partner who triangulates God against the other.

item.title: Ep 50 · Ch 15 — When the Disorder Becomes Part of the Cycle
item.description:
Chapter 15: Expanding the Practice of Emotionally Focused Therapy for Couples
Explores the "complex cycle," where depression, illness, or trauma fuses with a couple's pursue-withdraw pattern so each drives the other. Listeners learn why the therapist stops trying to fix the disorder and instead builds the attachment bond as a buffer against it.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 15: Expanding the Practice of Emotionally Focused Therapy for Couples
Explores the "complex cycle," where depression, illness, or trauma fuses with a couple's pursue-withdraw pattern so each drives the other. Listeners learn why the therapist stops trying to fix the disorder and instead builds the attachment bond as a buffer against it.

item.title: Ep 51 · Ch 15 — The Unchanging Focus: Affect, Slowness, and Presence
item.description:
Chapter 15: Expanding the Practice of Emotionally Focused Therapy for Couples
Distills EFT's constant across every case type into three pillars: working with attachment affect, slowing the process, and holding a present-moment focus. Listeners get concrete moves for staying on emotion when escalation, logistics, or trauma content pull them off-balance.
item.itunes:author: The Emotionally Focused Casebook
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 15: Expanding the Practice of Emotionally Focused Therapy for Couples
Distills EFT's constant across every case type into three pillars: working with attachment affect, slowing the process, and holding a present-moment focus. Listeners get concrete moves for staying on emotion when escalation, logistics, or trauma content pull them off-balance.
